behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff (file discovery/list -> numeric sort -> execution order). A pure Unit sort test can miss bugs where the runner reorders before execution. Higher levels rejected: System is disqualified because behavior text only requires order by filename prefix, not real OS events/network/subprocess timing; Agentic/Workflow are disqualified because no AI step exists.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test migrations directory with out-of-order filenames (e.g., 1_init.sql, 10_late.sql, 2_mid.sql). Run the migration runner against a local test Postgres and assert side effects prove apply order is 1,2,10 (e.g., each migration inserts its version into an audit table in execution sequence).

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run.
behaviors.justification: Real failure is state handoff between migration executor and _migrations metadata persistence across runs. Unit tests of skip logic can miss contract errors (wrong lookup key/version). Higher levels rejected: System is disqualified because behavior text is about persisted applied-state, which a local DB integration test reproduces without real network/OS primitives; Agentic/Workflow not applicable.
behaviors.test_description: Run runner once with a migration that has visible side effects (e.g., insert a marker row). Run runner again against same DB. Assert marker side effect count did not increase and _migrations contains one record per version.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted.
behaviors.justification: Needs real transactional interaction between runner, SQL execution, and metadata writes; mocks can hide rollback/partial-commit bugs. Higher level considered: System, rejected because behavior text is DB transaction semantics and stop-on-error flow, which is directly testable with local Postgres integration (no external OS/network/subprocess condition required). Agentic/Workflow not applicable.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare migration N with multiple statements where one fails after an earlier write, and migration N+1 with an obvious side effect. Execute runner and assert: writes from N are absent (rolled back), N is not recorded in _migrations, N+1 side effect is absent, and runner reports failure.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires concurrent starts from separate processes. Race conditions around locking/uniqueness/transaction isolation can be hidden by in-process fakes or single-process integration harnesses. Higher levels rejected: Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text (no AI/ML decisions).
behaviors.test_description: Launch two real runner processes simultaneously against the same Postgres and same migrations directory. After both exit, assert each migration version appears once in _migrations and each migration side effect is present exactly once.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is initialization ordering across modules (bootstrap metadata table then execute files) against DB state. Unit call-order checks can miss real DDL/execution ordering issues. Higher levels rejected: System is disqualified because behavior text is first-run DB bootstrap ordering, reproducible with local DB integration; Agentic/Workflow not applicable.
behaviors.test_description: Start from a fresh Postgres database with no _migrations table. Include a migration that would fail if _migrations creation had not occurred first (or capture SQL order via DB-visible effects). Run runner and assert _migrations exists and first migration completed, with metadata insert succeeding.
